speaker: interviewee
text: So, uh, then the couple of days after the, uh, what we called Mad Tuesday. The Mad Tuesday-- that's when these people all arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after the 17th of September. The 17th of September was the, uh-- actually where it started off. That's where the landings took place behind them. And, um, I think, uh, towards the end of September, that's when we got those first transports coming in? I don't know exactly the date. But then, uh, well, these people were behaving themselves very badly. They took possession of the whole camp, and that's what the Germans didn't like at all. And especially Gemmecker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the camp itself, then the Jewish people became afraid of those-- those, uh, National Socialists. And on the other side, the, uh, Dutch people thought that they could do even better in handling the Jews than the Germans were doing. So what Gemmecker did as a protection for the-- his control, the Jewish people-- he put a-- a barbed wire fence inside the camp to close off the Jewish section. How many Jews were left then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was about 300, 400. What did [? you said? ?] I don't know exactly the number. So-- and on-- on that spot, the opening into that section, there was a Jewish one taking the watch. That no-- none of the, uh, [? Anawayis ?] could enter there. Which the [? Anawayis ?] didn't like at all, because now they were told by a Jew that they couldn't enter that part of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was strife going on between the Germans, the Jewish people, and those people. And they-- handled handled themselves [INAUDIBLE] very badly. They put on fires just in between the barracks. There was burning of stuff-- what was parts of camp furniture and so on, and just burn it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that same fellow showed me the bullets, what he wanted to put through my [INAUDIBLE]. He said, gee, these people are even worse than the Communists. But they got rid of them very quickly. It was in three weeks' time they were transported over to, uh, the Luneburg Heide in Germany. And that's where they stayed up until the end of the war. Then we were on our own again. He said, so, now, happy that that's-- business is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gemmecker and [? Slazinger ?] were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, [? Slazinger ?] was there until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, yeah. Uh, at the-- the last months were a bit, uh, yeah, strange altogether. We didn't know is-- there was no transport anymore. And so that business was all over. Because there was a general strike in Holland. There were no trains going at all anymore. So they couldn't move anything. Only if there was a train on them, it was a German train. But nothing from Holland anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that stopped altogether. And we went through winter. And then there came a little tram, more or less-- smaller wagons-- into the camp, where the Germans were collecting their own stuff-- own stuff and what they had stolen from the Jewish people. There were all together, I think, 40 wagons-- something like that. It was quite a long train.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That train moved out shortly before the, uh-- the end of the war, to [? Hronia. ?] And we found that train back again in one of the suburbs of [? Hronia ?], Adure, untouched. And brought it later on back in again after the liberation into the camp so they couldn't take it over to Germany anymore. We unloaded that into the camp again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you still continuing with your underground activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes, very much so. That-- just that last particular period, I became very much more involved in the business. And, um-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had-- that's-- after that train left the camp, I got the message that I was going to take over the camp. The people in the camp asked me if I was willing to take over, but I thought, oh, better talk over with my wife what I'm going to do. Because I was already too much involved in any other things. And to run further risks. But, uh, became-- yeah. The last nine months of the war were the worse one for me. That I could hardly eat anymore. I couldn't take any solids anymore. So I was [INAUDIBLE] actually milk only.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, um-- then we had on the 8th of April-- that was from the Saturday, 7th or the 8th? 8th was Monday. It was a Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, it was on a Sunday night that the-- the Allied dropped [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 20, 30 people around the camp. And I got to know it before the evening-- before the middle of the night. And they knocked at my door, and they said, uh, there are, uh-- Canadians were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Landed around here. Could you take care of some? And they got six persons there. Yeah, what can I do with six? [? We had no ?] room. I couldn't do anything at all. So I thought the only thing I can do is bring them into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I brought them through the [? garage ?] into the camp to an empty barrack. And it was opposite where the, um, Germans had their, um-- their barrack. These elderly people, the [GERMAN]. And I said to them, OK, you are here. And I can't do anything for you. You have to take care about yourselves. They said to me, uh, don't you worry. We've got our rations, and we can look after ourselves well. I said, as long as you are not making yourself visible for the-- these Germans. And this was happened, and they have stayed there until the liberation. And after that, they joined their own group again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated on the 12th. But before that time, on the Sunday, we went-- went to church outside, and that's where we saw more action of the Germans. They were trying to search and find some more of-- of these, uh, parachutists. And they found another five or six. And these were arrested and put into-- we had a prison in the camp. That was barrack number 50. And they stayed there for a couple of days, and then they were transferred to [? Leewater. ?] But these were the only ones found. I know there was a Dutch captain with them. And they had practically walked over him, because he had buried himself a bit there in-- into the, uh, forest. Forest was just growing. And, uh, they never found him. So he was the first one who told me after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the 11th, it became unbearable. On-- on the 11th, that's where we know-- we knew that the Canadians were already close. And it was in between us and the township of Westerbork, a canal. And there were a couple of bridges over that. And some of them had already been blown off, and there was only one left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, um, that's where we thought, well, um, there's only one thing what we have to do. And that's just a split second reaction again. I said to my wife, OK, after this-- what happened, I have to go and see and come to the other side. That was then on the 9th-- 9th, on a Monday. Yeah. And I had a pass for Kamp Westerbork to enter it myself. And I went to-- to Westerbork, because I knew that Westerbork itself was already liberated at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew that [? Ivan ?] [? Brits ?] was still in [INAUDIBLE]. And there was a German soldier watching. I said, can I get across? He said, uh, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, here. And I showed my pass for Kamp Westerbork. He said, well, that must be a good one, then. Hey, he's on our side, he works there. And he let me go. And he-- he told me that I had killed just a boy of 17 years of age on the other side for treason. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For treason to attempt, not to [? assassinate. ?] When I went on the road further towards the, uh, township, there were farmers on the side we-- uh, whom we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to me, Mr. Van As, don't go. They are shooting there. I said, shooting? OK, why? He says, well, they are still fighting around it. I said, well, have they collected your milk this morning for the, uh, pasteurization of the milk? Said, yeah, he went over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, if he can go, I can go, too. So I drove further up. And there, I saw the first Canadian killed on the left-hand side of the road. Further into the township past the, uh, milk, uh, cooperative, by the hotel, so second one. And that's where I met another friend of the underground movement. And from there on, we started off talking about what we were going to do after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, uh, made known to them that I didn't want to have a Red Cross. What I said-- that, uh, we were going to be as soon as the Germans were leaving, then we were under control of the Red Cross. I said, no. No Red Cross, because I know what the Germans are doing. And they don't respect the Red Cross at all. So I said, I'm going to put on the, uh, [? bright ?] place, [? appelplatz. ?] And a cloth of, um-- Jewish stars, yellow stars, which were printed on it on the, uh, [? bright ?] place. That if then, they can see that we are, anyhow, a Jewish camp. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day thereafter, the Germans left on the 11th and by six o'clock, half past six that evening. And that's where they start shooting. The whole day, they were sitting outside doing nothing. It was beautiful day, sunny. And, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to go in-- into the camp again, because by that time, we were living outside camp, to my office. And I had my office just facing his office. So I could see-- see through the window what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, um-- as soon as I was inside the camp, I got the order, 'don't get out anymore. You can't, because we control this whole situation.' I thought, oh, gee, what's going to happen? Are they going at the last moment to kill something off? Or do something, uh, bad? And, um-- yeah. I send out my message girl-- that was a girl at the time-- to let my wife know what-- what was on. And she came to me and walked in, into the camp. I said, what are you doing here? Said yeah, but what's wrong with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, well, I can't get outside of the camp anymore, then they keep you into the inside as well. Said oh, no. They're not going to keep me inside. She says, OK. Then she left, but she [INAUDIBLE] moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I couldn't go back for having my lunch or having my tea. Just wait and see. And then at 6 o'clock about, that's where the shooting started from the other side, Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where slowly, the Germans packed in. They had their cars already waiting in front, and then they moved outside of the office. And that's where [? Slazinger ?] came. Outside of the camp, he had a telephone connection with the office in-- in, uh, outside, with-- with Gemmecker So he went over. And, well, I saw them talking to each other. And that's what, for the first time happened, and I've never seen before-- that's where they shook hands. Now, Gemmecker never touched any one of the Jews by hand. And that was for the first time. And he handed him something over, just a tiny [INAUDIBLE]. But I got to know later. Now, that's where they moved out. Slowly, they drove over and went in the direction of [? Hohal, ?] and from there to [INAUDIBLE], over to [INAUDIBLE] rest of them [INAUDIBLE] in Amsterdam. And he went straight away. After he saw them leaving, [? Slazinger ?] watched them. And as soon as they were out of sight, he moved over to my office and handed me over what he had gotten by Gemmecker to him. And that was a pistol-- just a tiny pistol. Just the size of your hand, pocket one. About 4.3 millimeter, something like that. A tiny one. And in that fact, he actually handed over the command to me. And from there on, the Germans, as soon as the, uh-- the staff moved out, the watch people-- the [GERMAN] Polizei opened the gate and went to the barracks. And, um, that's where I took over and put my men, the Jewish people, and closed the gate again. And I went straightaway into conference with, uh-- I said I will do it, to take over the command. But I'm still young, and I don't want to do it on my own. So I appointed straightaway six elderly people as my advisors around me. And with those people, I went into a-- a meeting, and that took the whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And well, my wife was still on the outside. And so I brought her in, because we had quite a lot to do. These German people were-- which were in the inside of the camp still-- those [GERMAN] Polizei-- what was I going to do with those people? So I had the commander there, and we had further discussions. And as they said in that period of time, we had 120 non-Jewish people in the camp also. And he said to me, uh, that he had instruction from the German commander to take these people out when they were leaving the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, oh, no. You are not going to take anyone out of the camp. If you want to leave, OK, that's your business. And I am not going to keep you in here. Because I didn't want to have German soldiers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was quite a discussion. It went on until practically half past 11:00. And that's where I gave in [INAUDIBLE]. I said, OK, um, you can take them. But if one hair is well, we say one hair. If-- if there is something going to happen to these people anyhow, I keep you responsible for it, hm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which-- to which he agreed, and they moved out shortly after 12 o'clock. And that's where we got full control of the camp. And I said, OK, now I'm going to give weapons to my men. These were all people who had carried weapons before, which were knowing how to handle weapons. And, uh, that's where we got the full control of the camp. And that took us the whole day thereafter. I didn't sleep a wink over the night. Because the next morning, as soon as I moved out of the meeting and I gave my first instructions to the camp, what was going to happen. So that circular was, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typed and multiplied. And [INAUDIBLE] I came outside. I [? know ?] that one came to me, and he shook my hands with both hands. He said, we are free! We are free! I said, we are not free yet. Be careful. And, uh-- no, they were quite happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, that took the whole day. Not that long. But in the meantime, yeah, well, everybody was happy. There were no people around them anymore, and we were, yeah, waiting and seeing what was going to happen. And there was a reconnaissance, uh, plane flying over the camp the whole day. And that-- that was directing the fire from these, uh-- two or three. And we had Germans walking around. So I had, uh, also men man the, uh, watchtowers. And these were telephone connected. And the telephone-- I got all the messages in. There are so many Germans walking on the east side, and there are so many walking on the west side. All the movements were directed to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I had a good connection with the-- the leader of the camp-- the farm. And I asked him to come into my office. I said, look, it becomes quite hectic in the camp, and if I can't control it anymore, can you go over and get me the Canadians here? He said, OK, I will do so. So we made the-- the distance from us walking towards the canal was about an hour. I said to him OK, it's now 11 o'clock. If you are going now, leaving now, and you can't get across, then I know you will be back at 1 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you are not there at 1 o'clock, then I'm going to instruct the people to see me in the hall-- in the big hall. Then I want to talk to them by 3 o'clock. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what I did. We got them altogether in-- in the hall. And I spoke to them. I said, look, uh, behave yourself. It doesn't take long anymore. But don't let the Germans know who are walking still around the camp what's going on inside-- inside the camp. And then I can't control if they are coming in and doing some harm to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh, that's where they said to me-- there was a question asked, what can we do on the-- keep the-- that's what I said. Keep the whole organization going as it is as much as possible. And we have to eat, and all these things like that. He said, yes, but what's going to happen if it takes longer? And can we then keep our Sabbath? I said, of course. Who wants to keep the Sabbath, they can keep the Sabbath. But I expect that if they are keeping the Sabbath, that they will [? give ?] their, uh, help on the Sunday that all-- everybody can get their [? meal. ?] And just finished that sentence. Then from behind, in the hall, someone called out and said, Mr. Van As, there's telephone for you. I said, telephone for me? The whole day, the telephone was ringing. So I said, yeah, what's on?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, the Canadians arrived! Well, approximately 1,000 people by the time in the hall. And they got to know at once that the Canadians were there, so they jumped out of the windows! [LAUGHS] And they walked over to the street. And my wife was just walking outside. She was nearly toppled over. And she was quite pregnant at the time from our son. So they ran out towards the farm, and that's where they were picked up. And then the, uh, commander-- that was Captain Morrison-- came to me. And we together went to the office where-- where-- the office of Gemmecker was before. And he pulled out of his, uh, briefcase a box with all cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And started to ask me about, is that person there? Is that person there? Because he knew-- he had instructions to approach me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whilst we were busy doing that, a group of Jewish people [? came on. ?] And, uh-- they, uh, said, Mr. Van As, Mr. Van As, we-- we want you here. And what they had a Dutch flag there, and they had an Oranje flag there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got me outside and, uh, if I wanted to hoist the flag. And I hoisted the flag, then, yeah. By singing the national anthem. Yeah, it was, uh, quite a story. Which national anthem?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they put a sash around me, Oranje-- Oranje sash. That's in, uh, [NON-ENGLISH]. Later on, they painted on it the date of the liberation. And actually, what's on there is the flag is mast at half. And the reason for it, on that particular day, President Roosevelt died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's in, uh, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Westerbork after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up until the end of March 1946. But, uh, I had-- but I was asked straightaway after the liberation that by the Dutch commander of the province if I would take over the control of the camp. Also for the, uh, Dutch [INAUDIBLE]. I said, no. I came here for the Jewish people, I leave with the Jewish people, and I see that that's the finish of my task here. No, I don't want to be part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you see as your task? When you say 'finish off your task?'

speaker: interviewee
text: That I saw it as my task. I came there for the Jewish people and to protect them. And that task was over on the moment that I brought them back home. So what was the use for me staying in the camp? I didn't want to control these-- [? Anawayis, ?] these [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people who had been to other camps come back to you when you were-- when you were still in Westerbork? People who had been sent out of the camp? Did you ever see anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes, several. Several had been back to-- and came to me. The first one, uh, coming into the camp-- that they came to me. Um, and we had several. One of them was Professor [? Kirido. ?] As soon as he came back from [? Theresienstadt, ?] he came to my place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they people that you'd saved? Were they people that you saved-- helped save their lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was [? Kirido. ?] That was one of the-- the one from whom I got a message from England to get him out of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people's lives would you say that you saved in the time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, uh, I can't say offhand how many. But uh, altogether, I think about 30, 35 in that area. But there were many more, many more. But I don't-- or I didn't count them. It was just a matter of you get your message for keeping that one out or trying to get that one out. I-- for instance here, there's also a thing. Getting people out of the camp. This was a typical fellow. He worked at my office, and he was a lawyer. But he was a bit, uh-- yeah, uneasy for himself, too. And he didn't know actually how to act. So you had to tell him practically everything. What to do, how to, what to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had, then, the green grocer was coming into the camp with a truck. Who brought every week the, uh-- the vegetables in. And they had a lot of crates.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the only way to get him out, because leave him on his own, and he absolutely went wrong. So his brother was there, too. And he was a far more acting person than he was. And, uh, he said to him, OK, dress yourself. Get your stuff that you want to take with you, and we'll build you in-- in the truck, in between the crates. Stand up. And he was standing just on the side of the railroad line, watching. [LAUGHS] Even if the Germans had come in at that particular time, they will say-- because fully dressed, hat on, and so on. And waiting for the time that he was called in to be built in-- into the crates. Well, that happened, just-- and nothing happened there particularly. So he went outside the camp and was collected by a friend of me. And he brought them over to the place where he stayed. Actually, he stayed in the house next to where he had lived all his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that friend of yours part of the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought them over. Yeah. And that was the one who also, I contacted on the, uh-- day before-- or when I went through the lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Baynard ?] [? Deboburg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you left the camp, uh, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, well, I got, uh, then transferred to the Department of Public Works. And that's where I got a house in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there from the 1st of April 1946 until um, July-- no, the 25th of October, 1955. That's the date we left for Australia. '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you go to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that was not particularly [? on. ?] Um, Australia right after the war-- the first thing-- when that, uh, officer, Officer [INAUDIBLE], asked me to take over the camp also for these, uh, [? christenings, ?] and I refused, I said, I want to do one thing. I want to go to Indonesia to help to liberate my parents, from whom I haven't heard anything either. So, uh, he took care of that, that I could go over, um, to Indonesia. And I was medical examined outside in August after the, uh-- the last transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I took over to the west. And when I came back at home, there I found my wife in bed stiff-- completely stiff. I said, what's wrong with you? 'I really don't know.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK, we'll go over and see the doctor first before I'm going over to Indonesia. Because my transport was booked for Indonesia on the 5th of October, 1945. It's the [? Shebayak. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- that was the ship I should-- or had to be go to Indonesia. So we went over to see doctor in [? Australia ?]. He said, well, let's make some x-rays, because we don't know if it is rheumatism, or if it is, uh, TB. I said, TB? Oh, that sounds bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened actually-- she was, uh-- she had the flu. And the doctor came out to see her, and he came from an [INAUDIBLE] TB patient. That's what we later found out. And he looked into her mouth, and that's how he transferred the, uh-- the sickness. And her reaction was, and that's what they called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] That was a prompt reaction. She was healthy enough to fight the, uh, infection straightaway. And that gave her, uh, all stiffening up in the joints. She couldn't move herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, she was under control of the, um, [INAUDIBLE] of the TB. But they said, yeah, now we know what she has. But what about you? And they showed him. So the doctor advised me not to go to Indonesia, and they took me off the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we under control for [? 32 ?] years before they said, OK, now you are safe, too. It could have happened that one of us had, uh, develop a-- a hole in one of the lungs. And we-- so, but doesn't happened that way. It was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break for the end of the tape there.
